policy_statement: Implement and establish systematic work to reduce the climate impact of food and meals in City operations (2024–2026).
mechanism_description: The City implements systematic work to lower the climate impact of food and meals in its operations, reducing emissions associated with food purchasing and consumption.
primary_causal_pathway: Institutional purchasing standards → diet/supply-chain shift to lower-emissions foods (+ less waste) → ↓ agricultural CH₄ + N₂O + CO₂ (incl. land-use change) → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) System changed: Public and institutional food procurement (menu design, sourcing set, vendor contracts, purchasing volumes), sometimes coupled with regional food-system infrastructure/network development to enable compliant sourcing.  
2) Mediating process: Demand-pull shifts production mix and supply chains toward less emissions-intensive foods (e.g., reduced beef/lamb, more plant-forward meals; potentially improved farming practices/inputs; reduced over-ordering and increased donation), lowering enteric fermentation and manure methane, fertilizer-related nitrous oxide, energy use in processing/transport where relevant, and emissions from land conversion and food waste.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in food-system lifecycle GHG emissions attributable to procurement-driven changes in consumption and sourcing.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced lifecycle GHG emissions from food production and consumption (especially methane and nitrous oxide, and avoided land-use change) due to lower-emissions procurement choices.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Procurement policies primarily act by changing food demand and sourcing, which reduces lifecycle GHG emissions from agriculture, land use, and waste—i.e., direct mitigation via emissions reduction.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While some programs also improve nutrition, equity, local economies, or resource efficiency, the mechanism’s climate-relevant centerpiece is lowering food-system emissions rather than primarily reducing energy/water use (Resource Efficiency), reducing climate hazard exposure (Adaptation), or enhancing ecosystems for sinks/buffering (Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: “Local” procurement can sometimes increase transport emissions, but transport is usually a smaller share than production-phase emissions; the dominant intended and typical impact remains emissions reduction from dietary and production-practice shifts. If a procurement policy were solely about reducing packaging or water use without changing food emissions, it would be a different mechanism.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances: All policies sharing this mechanism (sustainable food procurement frameworks and enabling supply-chain actions) operate through demand-side purchasing shifts that target lower-emissions food-system outcomes; thus Mitigation is consistently primary.  
5) Secondary subtype note: Any secondary tagging (e.g., health co-benefits, local economic development, waste reduction specifics) requires policy-level evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If “pro-climate” procurement is defined mainly as local sourcing without explicit diet/production-emissions criteria, net GHG benefits can be ambiguous; however, the canonical mechanism as stated explicitly targets food-system emissions reduction, keeping Mitigation dominant.
key_indicators: “reduce the climate impact”; “food and meals”; “in City operations”; “Implement and establish systematic work”
co_benefits: Public health/nutrition improvements; reduced food waste; potential biodiversity/animal-welfare benefits; local economic development (if local sourcing is part of implementation)
instance_edge_case_notes: Text is truncated and does not specify the concrete tool (e.g., formal procurement standard vs targets vs reporting). Classified as procurement-requirement because “City operations” and food/meals typically operationalize through purchasing/contracting and menu requirements; could shift to target-setting or information-disclosure if the full text specifies emissions-per-meal targets or supplier/menu carbon reporting.
